title: Why the job got heavier
paras: Let's start with why the job got heavier. Every planner I've ever known has, at some point, felt buried. That isn't a sign you're bad at the job. More often it's a sign that you're conscientious, that people trust you with the difficult files, and that the work has quietly grown faster than anyone reorganised the day to cope with it. Managing workload isn't a knack a few lucky people are born with. It's a set of habits you can learn — and the sooner in a career you learn them, the longer and more enjoyable that career tends to be. What follows isn't theory. It's what has kept me, and a good many planners I've worked alongside, on top of the work over four decades, rather than the other way round.

It's worth being honest that planning has become genuinely more demanding — because if you think the pressure is a personal failing, you'll manage it badly. A single application now attracts a longer list of statutory and non-statutory consultees than it did a generation ago. Ecology, drainage, heritage, highways, flood risk, nutrient neutrality, biodiversity net gain and viability can all sit on one modest scheme, each with its own report, its own consultant and its own timetable. Policy has thickened at every level, national and local, and it changes often enough that staying current is itself a task. Committees and the public scrutinise decisions more closely — and quite rightly. But that scrutiny has to be answered in writing, in detail, and on the record.

On top of that, expectations about speed have shifted under everyone's feet. A client who once accepted that a considered letter took a week now sends a follow-up email the same afternoon, and wonders why you haven't replied. Local authority teams carry all of this with fewer planners than they had ten years ago, so the work per head has risen even as the work itself has grown more complex. None of this is your fault — and saying so isn't an excuse. It's the necessary starting point, because you can't manage a workload sensibly while secretly believing a better planner would find it easy. A better planner would find it hard, and would have a system. This article is about the system.

Here's one from the file — a day that ran itself, and a day I ran. Early in my career, I let the telephone and the inbox decide the order of my day, and I went home most nights having been busy for nine hours with very little finished. The change that mattered was small, and slightly embarrassing in its simplicity. I began each morning by deciding, on paper, the one report or letter that had to be finished that day — and I did an hour of it before I opened my email. The work that used to slip for weeks started getting done. And the emails, it turned out, waited perfectly happily until nine o'clock.

title: The interrupted day
paras: Now, the interrupted day. The real enemy of planning work isn't volume so much as fragmentation. The job is full of live matters that all feel urgent at once, and each one generates interruptions. The agent who rings about the barn conversion while you're drafting a committee report on something else entirely. The email that pulls you off an appeal statement. The colleague at your desk with a quick question that turns out not to be quick. Each interruption feels small. But the cost isn't the two minutes it takes — it's the ten minutes afterwards, spent finding your place again in a complex argument you'd half-built in your head. Do that a dozen times, and a day that should have produced a finished report produces three half-finished ones and a headache.

The planners who cope are the ones who stop treating every interruption as equally deserving of an instant answer. Most aren't. The agent's question about the barn will keep for an hour; the committee report is due tomorrow and needs an unbroken run at it. Learning to hold that line politely — "I'm on a deadline this morning, can I call you back at two" — is one of the most useful professional skills there is, and it's entirely compatible with being helpful. Being available to everyone, all of the time, is not a virtue. It simply means the loudest voice sets your priorities. And the loudest voice is rarely the most important matter on your desk.

title: Deciding what matters first
paras: So how do you decide what matters first? When several things are pressing, the instinct is to start with whatever shouted last, or with whatever is quickest, so you can feel the satisfaction of clearing something. Both instincts are wrong often enough to be dangerous. The better discipline is to sort the day around two questions. What has a genuine, external deadline? And what carries the most consequence if it goes wrong? Planning is unusual in giving you a lot of hard fixed points to build around — determination dates, committee cycles, appeal timetables, consultation periods — and those are the skeleton of any sensible plan. A statement due at the Inspectorate on Thursday is not negotiable. A report for a committee three weeks away is important, but it can wait a few days without harm.

Beyond the fixed dates, separate the genuinely important from the merely urgent. A quick email demanding a same-day answer is urgent, but often trivial. A difficult judgement on a contentious application isn't urgent today, but it will shape the outcome of a scheme worth far more than a hundred emails. Give the important-but-not-urgent work protected time before it becomes urgent-and-panicked, because everything is easier to do well when it isn't also being done in a rush. Here's a simple version of this that works. Each morning, before the inbox, write down the two or three things that must genuinely move that day, and treat everything else as optional until those are done. A short, honest list of what actually matters beats an elaborate system you never keep up.

title: The inbox, and other people’s expectations
paras: Which brings us to the inbox, and other people's expectations. Email deserves its own section because, for most planners, it's where the day is quietly lost. The trap is treating the inbox as a live feed to be watched, so that every arriving message interrupts whatever you were doing. It's far better to treat email as a task to be done in blocks — two or three times a day, at times you choose, rather than continuously. Turn off the notification that pops up in the corner of the screen. Nothing good has ever come from being told, mid-sentence, that a new message has arrived. And when you do open the inbox, deal with each message once where you can. Answer it, file it, or note it on your list and move it out of the way — rather than reading it, worrying about it, and leaving it to be read again later.

Underneath the mechanics is the more important matter of expectations, and here planners often make a rod for their own backs. If you answer every email within the hour, you teach clients and colleagues that the hour is the standard, and they will hold you to it. Set the expectation you can actually sustain — early, and pleasantly. Tell a new client at the outset how you work. That you pick up messages at certain points in the day. That a considered answer on a planning question takes a little time. And that you'll always tell them when to expect a substantive reply. A short holding response — "received, I'll come back to you properly by Thursday" — buys you the time to do the thing properly. And, paradoxically, it leaves the client more reassured than a hasty half-answer would. Managing expectations isn't evasion. It's the difference between being a professional adviser and being a switchboard.

title: Good enough, and the perfectionism trap
paras: Now for good enough, and the perfectionism trap. Planners are, as a breed, conscientious — and conscientiousness has a shadow side. A great deal of time disappears into polishing work well past the point where the polishing changes anything. The instinct to make every document perfect is admirable and, applied indiscriminately, ruinous, because the effort you pour into gilding a routine report is effort stolen from the difficult appeal that genuinely needs it. The skill isn't lowering your standards. It's matching the effort to what the task actually warrants.

Some work has to be right to the last comma, because it will be read by an inspector, tested at an inquiry, or relied on in a legal context. An appeal statement. A committee report on a finely balanced major. A section 106 head of terms. These deserve real care. But a great deal of daily work doesn't carry that weight. A short delegated report on an uncontentious householder application needs to be clear, correct and proportionate — it doesn't need to be a small masterpiece, and the client isn't paying for one. Learning where the line falls — where "good enough" is genuinely, professionally sufficient, and where it isn't — is one of the marks of an experienced planner. The junior tends to give everything maximum effort, and burns out. The seasoned hand gives the right effort to the right things, and lasts. Knowing the difference isn't cutting corners. It's judgement — which is the thing we're actually paid for.

title: Getting work off your own plate
paras: Next, getting work off your own plate. If you manage or work alongside others, a good deal of workload trouble comes from doing things yourself that someone else could do — either because it feels quicker than explaining, or because you quietly believe no one else will do it properly. Both are false economies. The hour you save by not delegating today, you pay back many times over across a year of doing routine work that a more junior colleague could have handled — and, crucially, could have learned from. Delegation isn't offloading. Done well, it develops people, and a team that can share the load is worth far more than a single indispensable planner heading for exhaustion.

The trick is to delegate the whole of a defined task, with a clear brief and a genuine handover of responsibility — rather than half of it, under close supervision that costs you as much attention as doing it yourself would. Give the site visit and the first draft of the report to the assistant. Tell them what a good one looks like. Let them make a few mistakes you can correct while the stakes are low, and resist the urge to rewrite every line in your own voice. Even as a sole practitioner or consultant, the same principle applies to the specialists around you. Brief the ecologist or the highways consultant properly and let them own their piece, rather than trying to hold the whole of every discipline in your own head. Knowing what only you can do, and letting go of the rest, is not a loss of control. It's what control actually looks like.

title: Protecting the thinking time
paras: Then there's protecting the thinking time. The most valuable work a planner does — weighing a genuinely balanced case, structuring an appeal, thinking through a knotty policy conflict — needs uninterrupted concentration. And concentration is exactly the thing a busy office is designed to destroy. If you leave your thinking to whatever gaps survive the interruptions, you'll do it badly, late at night, when you're tired. Far better to treat concentrated work as an appointment you keep with yourself. Block out a period when you're at your sharpest, protect it as fiercely as you would a meeting with an important client, and use it for the one piece of work that most needs your best attention.

How you protect it matters less than that you do. For some it's the first ninety minutes of the morning, before the phones start. For others it's working from home one day a week, or booking a meeting room to escape the open-plan office, or simply telling colleagues that between ten and half past eleven you're not to be disturbed unless something is genuinely on fire. Turn off the email, put the phone out of reach, and give the hard task the run it needs. You'll get more done in that protected hour than in the three fragmented hours that would otherwise have swallowed the same work. And the difference in quality — a clear, well-structured piece of reasoning, rather than a patched-together one — is exactly the difference clients and inspectors notice, even when they couldn't tell you why.

title: Habits that last a career
paras: All of this comes down to habits that last a career. Everything above is really about building working habits you can sustain, because planning is a marathon and not a sprint. The planner who routinely works until eight, answers emails at midnight and never quite switches off isn't more committed than the one who works hard and then stops. They're simply closer to the point where the quality of their work, and their judgement, starts to fall away without their noticing. The warning signs of becoming overwhelmed are worth knowing, and worth taking seriously the moment they appear. Small mistakes creeping into careful work. Dreading the inbox. Deadlines that used to be comfortable suddenly feeling tight. A shortening temper. The sense of always being behind, however hard you run. None of these means you're failing. Each is a signal that the system needs adjusting, before the load does real damage to the work.

The response is rarely heroic effort — more often it's the opposite. Take the protected time more seriously. Be firmer about what's genuinely urgent. Hand more of the routine work to others. And be honest with clients and managers about what can realistically be done, and when. A sustainable pace isn't the enemy of good work; over any span longer than a fortnight, it's the precondition for it. The planners who are still sharp, still enjoying the job and still trusted with the difficult files after thirty and forty years are almost never the ones who worked the longest hours. They're the ones who learned — usually the hard way, and usually early — to stay in control of the work, rather than letting the work take control of them. That's a skill. It can be learned. And it's worth learning sooner rather than later.

title: Key lessons
paras: Let me finish with the key lessons — the habits that keep the work in proportion, gathered in one place.

First, the pressure is real, not a personal failing. Planning has genuinely grown heavier and more complex, with fewer staff. Manage it with a system, not with guilt. Second, protect against fragmentation, not just volume. The cost of an interruption is the time it takes to find your place again, and you don't owe every interruption an instant answer. Third, build the day around fixed points and consequence. Hard deadlines — determination dates, committee cycles, appeal timetables — are the skeleton. Give important work protected time before it becomes urgent. Fourth, write the short list first. Each morning, decide the two or three things that must genuinely move, and do the most important before you open your email. Fifth, treat email as a task, not a feed. Deal with it in blocks, turn off notifications, and handle each message once where you can. Sixth, set expectations you can sustain. Tell clients how you work at the outset — a holding reply that buys time for a proper answer beats a hasty half-answer. Seventh, match the effort to the task. An appeal statement deserves the last comma; a routine householder report needs to be clear and proportionate, not a masterpiece. Knowing the difference is judgement, not corner-cutting. Eighth, delegate the whole task, and let people learn. Doing everything yourself is a false economy that develops no one and heads towards exhaustion. Know what only you can do, and let go of the rest. Ninth, guard your thinking time. Concentrated work needs an uninterrupted block — book it with yourself, protect it like a client meeting, and do the hard task at your sharpest. Tenth, watch for the early warning signs. Creeping small mistakes, dreading the inbox, a shortening temper, always feeling behind — these are signals to adjust the system, not to run harder. And eleventh, pace it for forty years. A sustainable pace is the precondition for good work over a career, not the enemy of it. The planners still sharp at the end are rarely the ones who worked the longest hours.
